speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number 3, an interview with Mr. Henry Laurant. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Before moving on to Berlin, were there any incidents in Konigsberg that would reveal some real anti-Semitism that, that you can recall?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, at least one, one that I could remember very, very well because it really imprinted itself on my child mind. [SIGHS] I-- when I was about nine, maybe younger, I was-- I fell ill with diphtheria. And it was rather sudden. And my father took me to the hospital, which they-- where he was a consultant, a medical consultant. And outside of his private practice, he apparently was also a consultant to one or two hospitals and clinics. And the private rooms were all full. And so I had to go into the children's ward, the general ward, where there maybe-- I don't know-- 20, 30, 40 children, side by side. And at first, all was quiet. And there just sort of was the usual childish banter among them. I didn't know anybody, so I just lay there. I was too sick to take part, anyway. And then some bright spark looked on my nameplate and saw that I was Jewish. And that's when the fun began. And at the-- I don't think I spent a-- the, the, the decibel level in that room was such that nurses and even one time, a doctor, I remember, came in, indignant, you know, children, children! You  must-- you know. And when they heard, however, that they were-- all they were shouting was 'Christ killer' and-- this was around Christmastime, I should add-- and, and, and the various anti-Jewish things, then they were-- they became much more smiling [LAUGHS] and, oh, well, children will be children. However-- that was one. In other words, continuous jeering and, and shouting and insults, nothing physical. There were nurses on duty, and, well--

speaker: interviewer
text: When I left, that was almost the worst moment. Why? Because there was a glass cubicle at the end of the ward which had-- on top of a table, it had a children's bath. And everyone that left the ward had to be bathed in very antiseptic water, I suppose. And so I was, also.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was the first time that my friends in the ward had perceived me naked. And they saw, of course, that I was circumcised. [WHISTLES] [LAUGHS] Absolutely amazing what these children knew about foreskins and about the details of this, that, and the other. And, again, I have to add-- which I think is, I think, generally known, by this time-- that in Europe, people not-- unlike in America, men-- boys are not circumcised as a matter of course. In fact, in Germany at that time, certainly nobody was. Maybe in the meantime, they've gotten more hygienically conscious. But I doubt it very much because that is almost like a-- like a superstition. And so the only-- the, the-- it was your, your circumcisedness that betrayed you immediately as a Jew. There's a very interesting-- a very well-written story by-- and I wish I could remember the writer; it was published in America-- detailing the tribulations-- I think it's fictitious-- the tribulations of a boy in Poland, a Jewish boy in Poland, who, with his mother, hides out in different places, pretending-- the mother decides they don't look Jewish. So she pretends to be non-Jewish-- Catholic. And the fears that he goes through whenever he has to go into a public toilet, whenever he has to go anywhere. And a bath is out of-- out of the question, public showers, anything like that, out of the question. Because that's the best way-- if they're in doubt, let your pants down. That's it. And so this boy was living in-- I hadn't been, until then. I didn't know. Again, it was explained to me very-- [LAUGHS] I, I understood immediately, from what they were saying. I understood this immediately. And that really impressed itself on me. And my father did a very good job, however, of-- when I got home-- of explaining that they were the fools, not I. Because I would not be getting all these illnesses, et cetera, which collect-- bacteria-- which collect beneath the foreskin. And they would. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So I said, good! Good! I hope they do. [LAUGHS] So I just thought I would bring this-- it, it sounds like a nothing little incident. These things-- traumatic things happen to children all the time.

speaker: interviewee
text: But under these circumstances, in this kind of atmosphere-- constant atmosphere of fear and tension, that you-- and the sense of being in danger, even at that time, even in the early days of Nazism. Danger, at that time, well, meant being beaten up-- being wounded, maybe, you know. But, nonetheless, to a child, that is very serious.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you personally deal with the move itself? Were you--

speaker: interviewee
text: [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewer
text: --happy that you were moving away from Konigsberg?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe your--

speaker: interviewee
text: I think the whole family was very happy to be moving away. My sister-- I haven't said anything about the way that-- way that my sister suffered the same kind of discrimination as, as-- and treatment as I did at the boys' school. I should say that she did not. Girls are possibly somewhat softer. In this, they were less political. She was far, far more a part of the-- part of the group. She was-- she had this friend, Jimmy, who was very highly regarded by the others. And that protected her. So I think she was relatively better off. But even then, she had the feeling-- she knew what was happening in my case. She knew what was happening around. She was two years older than I.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, when we moved, she was-- I was 12. She was 14. We were reading newspapers like crazy, and, and you know, not-- the Jewish newspaper. There still was a Jewish newspaper. And so we knew what was going on. So we were totally in agreement that, that the place to go was Berlin because there, you can kind of get lost, we hoped. And for a while, this was-- this was true. And, of course, that coupled with the school situation there, which would be just 100% better-- Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: --this was-- this was quite-- was borne out, for a while, to be quite true.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that a coed school, Goldschmidt's?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, it was the first time that both of us, either one of us, had been in a coed school.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was exclusively for Jewish children?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was created for Jewish children. But I think there may have been some others, as well, some, some anti-Nazis, some foreign-- I, I don't-- I can't really answer that very well. I have a list. Rudy Goldschmidt, now Goldsmith, the son of Leonore, lives in London. And, and he-- and I have contact with him. He gave me, recently, a listing of all the alumni of the-- of the school that he had put together. And I'll, I'll look there. But I can't-- I've-- I looked through it, but not in detail. But I can't remember any foreign names. And I can't quite imagine that any non-German-- non-Jewish German would send their children there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any religious training at the school?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I'm afraid not, none at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you never went to cheder or anything of that nature?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did-- again, as I had done in Konigsberg, I went to the synagogue alone. And then, of course, came my 13th birthday, very shortly after-- well, a year-- a bit more than a year after moving to Berlin. So my preparation for the-- for the bar mitzvah was already-- that already put me much more in touch with religion and, and the synagogue than, than my parents ever, ever were in touch.

speaker: interviewer
text: But they supported your idea of--

speaker: interviewee
text: They did. They did, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a, a formal celebration of your bar mitzvah?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, indeed, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --it?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- at the synagogue, I, I had insisted on-- even though my Hebrew was kind of weak, I had insisted on reading, or singing, rather, a, a part of the-- an excerpt from the Torah, as I had seen others do. I wasn't going to do any less. And, and I had very good training for that. And I did. I got through it. And my father, whom I saw in the-- in the audience there, I don't think he came up. I don't-- I don't think he came up to the front part. But, in any event, he seemed to be-- it was a-- it was a funny mix of feelings because he was-- this was all very strange to him. Ideologically, he was opposed to it. He was areligious, maybe slightly anti, even though he never said that. But, but he was nonetheless impressed because he was conscious of being a Jew. And I don't think he ever reconciled-- poor man. This is-- this is-- this is one tragedy of many people that-- in his situation. I'm sure there were many in Germany because he is not the only German Jew that was-- that thought like he did. There must have been many-- to, to go to their deaths with those kind of basic questions about their own identity-- is, is a tragedy. Well, There's a tragedy, too. But this, this makes the tragedy even, even, even worse than-- Anyway, his expression was as joyful as I've ever seen it, and as joyful as it had been shocked when he saw the, the hate painting on our house. So this made me happy. And I think he was happy despite himself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because intellectually he certainly wasn't impressed, but I think it was impressed emotionally. And it was-- it was an occasion. And we had the usual party. And, and I got the usual presents-- 15 fountain pens, or, you know, how many watches? And your mother-- and your mother was equally as happy, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, my mother was--

speaker: interviewer
text: --more so?

speaker: interviewee
text: --was fully supportive. And she didn't have the intellectual baggage that my-- [LAUGHS] I don't mean to say that she had no intellect. She had a very sharp intellect. But it was not the kind of intellect-- somewhat more heavy, somewhat more studious-- as my father did. And 'ponderous,' I guess, is the word, without being offensive, but that is-- that is the kind of a-- it's a better word than 'heavy.' Because he was not heavy, but a kind of ponderous thinking. I've-- I've inherited some of that myself. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But she was much closer, in the sense of being closer to reality. And she saw that this was something that was tremendously important to me. And I had already, in the house-- in our house, introduced the, the, the Friday night-- Friday dinner. We-- I said the prayers. And my father put his hat on. And I put my yarmulke on. And, and they gave the responses, which I taught them.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS] Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so it was a-- really a kind of a-- I can't say rejuvenation because it wasn't something that was there before, but a kind of inculcation of and embracing of, of Jewish forms. Now, it was nothing more than forms for them. And they did it largely to do me a favor--

speaker: interviewer
text: And 'them' include--

speaker: interviewee
text: --because it was important to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that includes your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, I would say that she was-- followed more the areligious and not terribly Jewish attitudes and beliefs and emotions of my-- of my parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any, any attempt on your part to, to eat kosher food versus non-kosher food? Was that a--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --an issue--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it didn't go as far as that.

speaker: interviewer
text: In 1936 is when you arrived to Berlin, I think you said.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did there come a time when Hitler became a, a difficult problem for the Jews in Berlin itself?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a gradual process, I would say. He always had been a difficult problem. But he became a much more difficult problem after the Olympic Games were over. The foreigners had gone home. And he had to do-- Hitler had to do something to, to impress the, the populace. And what, what mainly-- the way I saw it, mainly-- the way it affected me, mainly-- is that I saw a multiplication of the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the, the-- not billboards, the display cases that were put up all over the city with the pages of the which was the anti-Semitic-- it was a publication. I think it was weekly. I don't think it was daily-- weekly. That was-- its purpose was to show the-- to warn the populace against how horrible the Jews are, and how they must hate them, and how un-German they are, and, and how good Germans, the readers, are, in other words, Jew-hating, Jew-baiting of the worst kind. All Jews that were illustrated in the drawings had rather hook noses. They vastly exaggerated features, et cetera, et cetera. All of them were leering at blond German maidens. And the usual-- I mean, it was just so full of stereotypes. It is just incredible, nowadays, to even think about. And there was a-- the, the head of that was Streicher, who, I'm happy to say, was hanged after the Nuremberg trials. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the-- there were others-- other things of that same kind. I was hit in a very-- in a very-- we were somewhat insulated, I will say this because my father had his psychoanalytic practice. They were not-- it wasn't exclusively Jewish, by any means. But he-- and he was trying to, to get more, of course. It was a very difficult time. We were living off capital, as I later learned. My mother had her art. At that time, you could still go to theaters-- Jews could still go theaters and enjoy-- participate in the-- in the cultural pursuits. And there were many in Berlin still at that time. I happen to love opera and theater, also. But I went to the opera house quite often and concerts-- Berlin Philharmonic and so on. And I was 12 and 13 at the time, so this was-- these were very impressionable years. And one time, in '38-- this was prior to the Kristallnacht, I remember-- was an opera performance that I very much wanted to see. And the curtain-- there was a delay in the curtain going up. And I was in my seat-- or, rather, standing. [LAUGHS] I had standing room. I obviously didn't enough money to buy a seat. I was standing up on the-- on the-- top floor. And the man comes out and says that-- in very hypocritical language-- says, all Jewish ladies and gentlemen are requested to please vacate the theater, since the-- since certain, certain party bigwigs are about to, to take their places in the theater. And they can't be in the same room-- unthinkable. And they will go to the box office and get their money back. And so I went to the box office, got my money back, which was actually not true, because all they did-- you gave them-- was the address. And they'd send you the money, which they never did. [LAUGHS] So even there, they screwed you.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was a blow to me because I had-- well, it's, it's a matter of refuges. You look for refuges. Where can you go that you aren't being-- aren't being well, at best, demoralized, at worst, beaten up, somewhere in-between humiliated, insulted, et cetera? Where do you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: And the opera house-- dark, anonymous in the audience-- had been one of those places. Now it was dangerous, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there--

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, after Kristallnacht, there was no more of that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a mass exodus from that opera house?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, very few. Very few.

speaker: interviewer
text: Very few.

speaker: interviewee
text: Very few. Very few went-- by that time, very few Jews went to it. And, in fact, I had some arguments with, with fellow students, with, with friends, you know. They said, you shouldn't go to those bastards', you know, those, those bastards' theaters.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I said, I'm not going to let them deprive me culturally. This is ridiculous. Why not? Until the-- until they won't let me, until they forbid me-- once they forbid it, I won't go. I won't pretend to be-- to be a-- to be a Christian. But why not? They couldn't give me a good reason why not. And so I went. You were not required to wear a yellow star at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, no, that came later. That came only with the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe the events leading up to Kristallnacht, as you remember it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, indeed. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] A-- let me-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] there was a Jewish family-- Polish Jewish family-- called vom Rath. No, I'm sorry. Strike that. The family was called Grynszpan-- Grynszpun or Grynszpan, I'm not sure which. The son was called Herschel. He managed to get out of Poland to France. He lived in Paris. He was a student. This is the story, as I remember it. I think it's fairly exact. The-- his parents-- there were no siblings, as far as I know-- the parents went to Germany and lived there. They were [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] rounded up. They were arrested by the Gestapo, sent to a camp, and were not heard of since. The young man, Herschel was informed of this through friends. It was peacetime-- mail, phone, whatever. And, and had a fugue-- he had a, a rage overcoming him. He borrowed a gun from somebody. He went to the German Embassy, asked to see somebody he didn't even know. The first high-looking, high-- official that looked as if he was a high-ranking official, he shot him. Then he gave himself up to the police. And he just was totally-- he was momentarily deranged, as I might have been, as anybody might have been. [DRINKS] This was-- I told you that, after the Olympics, after various other things that had happened-- '38 had been quite, quite a year. Don't forget, '38 had been the Anschluss with Austria. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there were a couple of other things. The Parteitag, the party, party convention, was particularly violent at that time. But he had to show-- give something. This was perfect. This was absolutely perfect for him. And so the propaganda people-- Goebbels, et cetera, and his henchmen-- they dreamed up a, a plot to, to assassinate this great patriot, vom Rath. Vom Rath was the name of the consular that was shot dead. And the German people was to arise in spontaneous punishment and, and rage against the, the Jewish oppressor and the-- in our midst. And that's what they did-- Kristallnacht, of course, because they smashed every window in sight-- of any Jewish shop in sight and many houses, and arrested thousands of, of men, put them in concentration camps. And I understand that they only were-- some of them, not all of them-- some of them were let out on the-- on the promise, A, to give their entire assets, make them over to the-- to the government, to the Reich-- and, B, to leave Germany. Now, it wasn't easy to leave Germany, I should add. At that time, as you know, there was no place to go. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did Kristallnacht affect your family, in particular?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went into hiding. We had some advance warning. These things were-- somehow, there was a very effective grapevine in Berlin. And, and Jewish circles got wind of what was going to happen. Besides, you didn't have to get wind of it. The way in which the press painted and covered this incident-- the, the, the assassination, the murder of vom Rath by, by Grynszpan-- was enough to make you pretty sure that something-- you didn't know what-- but something was going to come that was not pleasant. And so my father spent-- we left in-- I think the Kristallnacht was the night of November 10. And it was either the previous night, or I think it was the morning of that same day that--

speaker: interviewee
text: --1938-- that, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that the looting and everything started, and that we went-- my mother, my sister, and I went to the house of friends of ours, not far from our house that we were living in then, in Berlin, and hid out there. We were shown-- this was located in a sort of private park, or a large garden, if you will. And the gates had to be opened. Anybody that wanted to come in had the-- had to have the, the garden gates to open, so the drive-- so then they could drive to the house. So it wasn't easy to, to get there. I still, to this day, don't-- I have a false memory of this because the memory I have is of some friends of ours who were Jewish, who were called Simon and who had a house, something like that. My memory is that we were in that house. But it can't be because they were Jewish. Anyhow, that-- that's really beside the point. The point is that we were in hiding. And my father was-- we had no idea where he was. He was with friends. He didn't want us to know, in case we were asked and questioned in, in forceful ways. And so we didn't know where he was. And we saw each other again at our house, about four days later. And that is also when the regulations came into force about the professions that I mentioned before, about Jewish doctors and Jewish lawyers. I should also add that-- you were asking what other measures there had been, prior to that, that made life difficult. The many-- a large proportion-- in the '20s, a large proportion of the commercial life of the country was in the hands of Jews, one way or another. They were very smart. And their competitors weren't, as simple as that. And with hindsight, one could say, hey, that wasn't very smart of them, to be too, too, too dominant in any one field.

speaker: interviewer
text: But this is easier to say. I'm quoting, more or less, the words of my cousin, the man I mentioned before, who was doing the negotiating in, in Bonn. Did I mention him before? Yes, you did.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. And he, he mentioned something very, very similar. You know, he was there. He was an adult at the time. And, and he said there was-- there was-- this isn't a justification. This isn't any reason for the anti-Semitism. The anti-Semitism was there. But it was activated and made, made sharper by the fact that people were feeling economically advantaged by the Jews. Jews have distinctive names. And when, again and again, you see the head of businesses-- heads of businesses having Jewish names, it's not-- it doesn't help.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, anyway, this was, of course, something that the Nazis intended to, to, to radically change. And they did. And they-- you could hardly pass a Jewish shop in Berlin at that time which didn't have a large sign in the window-- [SPEAKING GERMAN], excuse me-- Aryanization. In other words, they were in the process of being Aryanized, which meant, usually, that the damned thing was confiscated by somebody, some party hack's cousin or something. Hey, there's a Jewish shop, there-- you know, we'll get that for you. What business would you like to be in? That's the way their world turned. And then they legalized it afterwards.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that, that sign meant that the lawyers were hard at work to find the-- to draw up the legal documents that would-- that would make this theft appear legal. There was always some money that changed hands, but--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you live--

speaker: interviewee
text: --not much.

speaker: interviewer
text: --anywhere near the area of the looting-- Kristallnacht?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm sorry-- did I--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you anywhere near that area where they were looting, during--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --that night?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, looting-- well, we were in a residential area at that-- at that time. So we weren't right next door to it, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see any of it, personally?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't see the actual looting. No, that's not true. I did the next day because some of it went over until the, the next day or maybe even later than that. What I saw was the next day, in the street. And that was something that, that was very, very hard to, to see. But it was harder for the people who was-- whose shops and whose-- there was-- I saw one piano that had been thrown out of the window of a-- of a Jewish apartment owner. And, and it was lying there, on the street, a Bechstein.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a sense-- or did you-- did you feel a sense of fear about the whole thing? Or was it just an adventure for you as a young man?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] No, no, it was not an adventure. It was fear from beginning to end. It was fear of, as I mentioned before, being harmed. I think I mentioned it in the context of being a child in Konigsberg. Being an adolescent in Berlin was worse because I was an adolescent, and I was half child, half man. I was therefore-- you beat up a child, it's a little bit less easy than beating up a young man. So the older I got, the more I realized I was vulnerable. Secondly, when you were out on the street, you had to keep a very careful eye out for people who either knew you, knew of you-- doesn't mean only the famous people. I mean, people from the neighborhood or whatever-- that have some idea, tradespeople, and so forth. And they talk an awful lot. And if they're party members and they-- oh, there goes the Jew, or something like that. But worst of all was in Berlin-- I didn't get this in Konigsberg. I don't think they were organized yet that way. Berlin-- they had troops of SA, stormtroopers, marching by, with the 'blood flag,' as they called them, which was the flag that had been sort of hallowed in the course of one of the Nuremberg party conventions and had been touched to the-- to the-- they have their heroes and their martyrs. And it had been touched to one of those flags.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is the end of tape number 3.
